what_it_does: An end-to-end acceptance script that exercises the structural route detector, writes one finding per real orphan route into an FsStore, seeds each catalogued orphan's disposition from the orphan-routes catalogue, and verifies the burn-down rollup reflects the live backlog. It also checks that re-detection does not inflate the model and that an operator-set disposition survives re-seeding.
observations: Driven by real project data rather than hardcoded fixture counts.
Computes expected accepted/open counts from the live detector output plus the orphan-routes catalogue.
Creates a temporary FsStore and calls cd.record_structural_findings and cd.burn_down.
Uses a hand-rolled check(label, cond) helper that asserts, increments a global PASS counter, and prints ok.
Tests catalogue-tag-to-disposition migration for a known voice route.
Tests operator disposition persistence across re-detection for /api/knobs.
Docstring states candidate detectors stay report-only, but the test body does not exercise them.
standouts: Derives expected counts from the same live detector and catalogue it tests, making the assertion logic self-aligning.
Explicitly guards that re-detection is idempotent and does not overwrite operator decisions.
Validates the full chain: detector → finding store → disposition overlay → burn-down rollup.
conventions: Long narrative docstring explaining the test's purpose and claims.
Inline assertion helper instead of a unittest/pytest framework.
Global mutable counter for pass tally.
concerns: Test outcome depends on the live state of the project (orphan-routes.json and route reachability), so it may be environment-sensitive.
Expected counts are computed from the same inputs the code under test uses, so count assertions are somewhat tautological rather than independently verified.
The candidate-detectors-are-report-only claim in the docstring is not exercised in the test body.
A file is opened with open(...) and passed to json.load without an explicit close.
Uses bare assert rather than a structured test runner.
notes: A live-data acceptance test that proves the orphan-route detector, finding store, catalogue disposition seeding, and burn-down rollup work together without inflating or clobbering operator decisions.
questions: What does cd.record_structural_findings return when there are no orphan routes?
How is FsStore initialized and where is its schema defined?
What fields does a disposition record contain beyond disposition, reason, and by?
Are candidate detectors tested elsewhere for the report-only behavior mentioned in the docstring?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the actual code: it wires the detector to the store, seeds catalogue dispositions, computes burn-down, and verifies idempotency and operator persistence.
apparent_themes: end-to-end acceptance testing on real data
intention_signals: Validate that the structural-detection pipeline correctly records real orphan routes, turns catalogue tags into initial dispositions, produces an accurate burn-down, and preserves human decisions across repeated detection runs.
novelty: An acceptance test that derives its expected counts from live detector output and the orphan-routes catalogue rather than static fixtures, and that explicitly tests disposition durability under re-detection.
contribution: A real-data integration harness that exercises the detector-to-store-to-burn-down flow and guards against duplicate findings and clobbered operator dispositions.
summary_for_embedding: End-to-end acceptance test that wires the structural route detector to the finding store and burn-down rollup, verifying real orphan routes are recorded, catalogue tags seed dispositions, and re-detection neither inflates the model nor overwrites operator decisions.
intention_for_embedding: Validate the finding-flow pipeline on live project data while ensuring idempotent detection and durable operator dispositions.

symbols.does: Asserts a labelled condition, increments a global pass counter, and prints an ok message; raises AssertionError on failure.
symbols.description: A lightweight inline test helper used throughout the acceptance script. It takes a human-readable label and a boolean condition, validates the condition, bumps the PASS counter, and emits a concise ok line so the script can report how many checks passed.
